perspectives.name: Westlake
perspectives.shortBio: Follow the march from Westlake toward Capitol Hill and the streets that became CHOP.
perspectives.fullBio: This route begins downtown, where people gathered before marching east toward Capitol Hill in June 2020. It follows the movement from Westlake through Pike/Pine toward Cal Anderson Park, connecting public grief, protest chants, and the city streets that led into the CHOP area.

perspectives.name: Alex
perspectives.shortBio: Alex’s CHOP route follows queer care work: checking on people, sharing supplies, and helping keep protest space safer.
perspectives.fullBio: Alex is an LGBTQ+ community organizer who helped hold space during CHOP through daily check-ins, resource sharing, and informal safety networks. Their route follows moments where queer care, mutual aid, and protest strategy overlapped in the streets around Cal Anderson Park during the summer of 2020.

perspectives.name: Jordan
perspectives.shortBio: Jordan’s route follows a nearby resident watching CHOP reshape ordinary blocks into contested public space.
perspectives.fullBio: Jordan lived just blocks from Cal Anderson Park when CHOP took shape. From her apartment and the surrounding streets, she saw neighbors, organizers, artists, medics, and visitors turn everyday blocks into a contested public space. Her route reflects a resident’s view of how protest reshaped daily life in Capitol Hill during the summer of 2020.

perspectives.name: Sam
perspectives.shortBio: Sam’s CHOP route centers Black witness, public grief, and the demand that police violence not be ignored.
perspectives.fullBio: Sam came to CHOP as a Black man carrying both exhaustion and hope. He had seen how quickly public grief could be dismissed, and he wanted to stand where people were refusing to look away. Along this route, Sam reflects on the weight of police violence, the relief of being surrounded by people demanding change, and the complicated feeling of watching a neighborhood become both a protest site and a place of care.

perspectives.name: Tiana
perspectives.shortBio: Tiana’s CHOP route follows mutual aid: food tables, supply runs, check-ins, and everyday care.
perspectives.fullBio: Tiana first came to CHOP with a backpack full of water bottles, snacks, and phone chargers. She did not think of herself as a leader, but she kept showing up where help was needed: carrying supplies, checking on strangers, sharing information, and making sure people had a place to rest. Her route tells the story of care work that often happened at the edges of the crowd, but shaped how the protest community survived day to day during CHOP.
